title: Capitalizing on Nonbanks: Regulatory Arbitrage Within Bank Holding Companies - Liberty Street Economics
gist: A large share of U.S. nonbank financial intermediation actually occurs inside bank holding companies, where lightly regulated nonbank subsidiaries hold a disproportionate share of the group's equity. Because these subsidiaries function as 'equity reservoirs' and equity is the dominant internal transfer channel, BHCs can reallocate capital across the firm to arbitrage tighter bank capital rules, a pattern that becomes visible after Basel III bound in 2015.

themes.theme: Scale of nonbank activity inside BHCs

insights.claim: Roughly one in four dollars of U.S. nonbank financial intermediation is conducted by an entity whose parent owns a regulated bank, a share that has grown steadily over time.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: A meaningful chunk of the 'shadow bank' sector is not actually outside the regulated perimeter but sits under bank parents, so investors and regulators mismeasure systemic risk if they treat nonbank and bank finance as separate silos.
insights.quote: roughly one in four dollars of nonbank financial intermediation today is conducted by an entity whose parent owns a regulated bank
insights.locator: BHCs Account for One in Four Dollars

insights.claim: Nonbank subsidiaries account for roughly 20 to 30 percent of consolidated bank-holding-company assets in the post-2010 period, with broker-dealers the largest single component.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: Bank balance sheets are far more nonbank-heavy than headline deposit franchises suggest, so credit and market-making exposures embedded in BHCs deserve scrutiny when assessing large-cap financials.
insights.quote: nonbank subsidiaries account for roughly 20 to 30 percent of consolidated BHC assets in the post-2010 period
insights.locator: The modern BHC is a complex organization

insights.claim: Operating a nonbank subsidiary is common even among mid-sized banks: roughly two-thirds of BHCs with $1 billion to $10 billion in consolidated assets run at least one nonbank subsidiary.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: Regulatory-arbitrage capacity is not confined to the megabanks; it is a structural feature across the regional-bank complex, broadening where the effect could show up.
insights.quote: roughly two-thirds of BHCs with between $1 billion and $10 billion in consolidated assets operate at least one nonbank subsidiary
insights.locator: The spike around the financial crisis

insights.claim: The 2008 conversion of Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley to bank-holding-company status produced a visible spike in nonbank assets held within BHCs.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: Structural shifts in charter status can mechanically move large blocks of activity inside or outside the bank perimeter, so time-series comparisons of nonbank penetration must control for such regime changes.
insights.quote: The spike around the financial crisis reflects the conversion of Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley to BHC status in 2008.
insights.locator: The spike around the financial crisis

insights.claim: Ninety percent of nonbank subsidiaries are under direct parental control, with 73 percent of subsidiary-quarters wholly owned and a further 17 percent majority-owned.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: High ownership concentration means parents have effective authority over subsidiary capital, dividends, and financing, the levers needed to reallocate capital across the firm.
insights.quote: 90 percent of nonbank subsidiaries fall under direct parental control
insights.locator: These subsidiaries operate under direct parental control

insights.claim: Prudential capital requirements apply to the depository institution and to the consolidated holding company but not to nonbank subsidiaries individually, so units under the same parent face very different capital constraints.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: The regulatory perimeter is drawn at the entity level, creating an arbitrage seam inside the firm that consolidated capital ratios alone do not reveal.
insights.quote: different subsidiaries operating under the same parent can face very different capital constraints
insights.locator: The Capital Is Not Where the Assets Are

insights.claim: The median nonbank subsidiary carries an equity-to-asset ratio of 69 percent versus roughly 10 percent for the median bank subsidiary, because nonbanks lack insured deposits and rely more on equity funding.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: Nonbank units are equity-rich relative to banks, so they act as a stockpile of deployable capital the parent can tap rather than raising fresh outside equity.
insights.quote: The median nonbank subsidiary has an equity-to-asset ratio of 69 percent.

insights.claim: The median nonbank subsidiary holds under 2.5 percent of consolidated assets but nearly 16 percent of consolidated equity, an 'equity multiplier' of about 6.5.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: The equity multiplier quantifies how concentrated redeployable capital is in tiny nonbank balance sheets, giving a metric to gauge a firm's internal arbitrage capacity.
insights.quote: The median nonbank subsidiary accounts for a little under 2 1/2 percent of consolidated assets but nearly 16 percent of consolidated equity, for a multiplier of 6.5.
insights.locator: As a result, equity within the organization

insights.claim: The equity multiplier is highest for funds and asset managers, while even nonbank lenders, the most leveraged category, carry a multiplier near 5.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: The arbitrage reservoir is deepest where balance sheets are lightest, suggesting asset-management arms inside banks are the richest source of redeployable equity.
insights.quote: even nonbank lenders, the most leveraged category, carry a multiplier near 5
insights.locator: The ratio is highest for funds and asset managers

themes.theme: Equity reallocation as the dominant internal channel

insights.claim: Within BHCs, equity reallocation, not liquidity reallocation, is the quantitatively dominant internal margin, with parent equity positions in banks exceeding non-equity positions by an order of magnitude.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: Most research focuses on intra-firm liquidity loans, but the real adjustment lever is equity, reframing how capital-rule changes transmit inside financial conglomerates.
insights.quote: equity reallocation is a quantitatively dominant margin within these organizations
insights.locator: Our evidence points to an additional channel

insights.claim: Parent equity investments in bank subsidiaries average 10 to 11 percent of bank assets over 2005-24, while non-equity investments total just 1 to 2 percent and net intra-affiliate borrowing stays near zero.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: The dominance of equity over loans in parent-to-bank funding shows capital, not liquidity, is how BHCs top up bank subsidiaries under pressure.
insights.quote: Parent equity investments in banks average 10–11 percent of bank assets over 2005–24, while non-equity investments such as loans and receivables total just 1–2 percent
insights.locator: Decomposing parent investments in bank subsidiaries

themes.theme: Basel III and the 2015 capital divergence

insights.claim: Beginning in 2015, when Basel III minimums became binding, banks affiliated with nonbank subsidiaries accumulated substantially larger excess capital buffers, with the gap versus banks without such affiliates widening to 8-10 percentage points by 2020.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: Access to internal equity reservoirs let some banks over-capitalize cheaply after Basel III, a competitive and structural edge that outside investors cannot see from consolidated ratios.
insights.quote: banks affiliated with nonbank subsidiaries accumulate substantially larger capital buffers, with the gap widening to 8–10 percentage points by 2020

insights.claim: No comparable capital divergence appears at the consolidated holding-company level, where excess capital declined gradually for BHCs both with and without nonbank subsidiaries as new requirements phased in.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: The arbitrage is invisible at the consolidated level, so relying on group-wide capital ratios understates how the effect is achieved by shuffling capital between entities.
insights.quote: Banks become better capitalized; the organizations that own them do not.

insights.claim: The authors argue much of the post-Basel III increase in bank capital came from equity reservoirs inside the organization rather than from outside investors, identified using historical interstate banking deregulation as variation in organizational complexity.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: If banks meet tighter rules by internal capital shuffling rather than fresh raises, capital regulation may be less binding on group risk-taking than it appears, a signal for how future rules should target the perimeter.
insights.quote: much of the increase in bank capital came from the equity reservoirs inside the organization and not from outside investors
insights.locator: In our next post, we show
